title: LLMs-as-Judges: A Comprehensive Survey on LLM-based Evaluation Methods
content:
Abstract: The rapid advancement of Large Language Models (LLMs) has driven their expanding application across various fields. One of the most promising applications is their role as evaluators based on natural language responses, referred to as "LLMs-as-judges". This framework has attracted growing attention from both academia and industry due to their excellent effectiveness, ability to generalize across tasks, and interpretability in the form of natural language. This paper presents a comprehensive survey of the LLMs-as-judges paradigm from five key perspectives: Functionality, Methodology, Applications, Meta-evaluation, and Limitations.

Problem: Traditional evaluation methods, including programmable metrics (e.g., BLEU, ROUGE) and human annotations, face significant challenges when applied to LLMs. Standardized metrics often fail to capture key aspects such as text fluency, logical coherence, and creativity. Human evaluation is time-consuming, resource-intensive, and difficult to scale. There is a need for flexible, interpretable, and scalable evaluation methods that can handle the open-ended and generative nature of LLM outputs.

Approach: This survey systematically reviews the LLMs-as-judges paradigm from five key perspectives: (1) Functionality: Why use LLM judges? - covering performance evaluation, model enhancement (reward modeling, verification, feedback for refinement), and data construction (annotation, synthesis). (2) Methodology: How to use LLM judges? - detailing single-LLM systems (prompt-based, tuning-based, post-processing), multi-LLM systems, and human-AI collaboration. (3) Applications: Where to use LLM judges? - exploring domains such as dialogue, retrieval-augmented generation, code generation, education, law, medicine, and finance. (4) Meta-evaluation: How to evaluate LLM judges? - discussing metrics (agreement, consistency, bias) and benchmarks. (5) Limitations and future directions.

Results: The survey finds that LLMs-as-judges offer significant strengths: flexibility to adjust evaluation criteria based on task context, ability to generate interpretable evaluations (explanations and feedback), and scalable and reproducible alternative to human evaluation, reducing costs and time. They have been successfully applied in performance evaluation, model enhancement (e.g., reward modeling, verification, self-refinement), and data construction (annotation and synthesis). However, challenges remain, including sensitivity to prompt templates (leading to bias or inconsistency), inherited implicit biases from training data, difficulty adapting standards dynamically to specific contexts, and issues with self-correction effectiveness without external validators.

Limitations: The paper identifies several limitations of LLMs-as-judges: (1) Evaluation results are often influenced by prompt templates, which can lead to biased or inconsistent assessments. (2) LLMs may inherit various implicit biases from their training corpus, impacting fairness and reliability. (3) Distinct tasks and domains require specific evaluation criteria, making it difficult for LLMs to adapt their standards dynamically to specific contexts. (4) Self-feedback and self-correction capabilities are often overestimated; LLMs often fall short of effectively improving reasoning quality without reliable external validators. (5) LLM judges do not always satisfy transitivity in pairwise comparisons, leading to concerns about reliability and trustworthiness. (6) There is a risk of data leakage and evaluation bias from static benchmarks.
